title: Improve activation for a new personal finance app
prompt: A personal finance app has strong signups but only 28% of new users connect a bank account in the first week. What would you do?

title: Design a product for new parents returning to work
prompt: Design a product that helps parents returning to work after parental leave. Who would you build for first, what jobs-to-be-done would you target, and how would you know it worked?

title: Design a product to prepare for a citizenship test
prompt: Design an end-to-end experience that helps an immigrant prepare for and pass a national citizenship test. Where is the biggest unmet need, and what would you ship first?

title: Improve content discovery for a streaming service
prompt: A major streaming service finds that 30% of sessions end without a play because users cannot decide what to watch. What would you investigate and change?

title: Build trust signals for first-time marketplace buyers
prompt: On a peer-to-peer marketplace, first-time buyers convert 4x lower than repeat buyers. How would you raise first-time buyer trust without making the product feel heavy?

title: Diagnose day-1 churn in a meditation app
prompt: A meditation app sees 60% of new users never return after the first session. What hypotheses would you test, and which would you validate first?

title: Order frequency drops in week 4 for grocery delivery
prompt: A grocery delivery app sees order frequency fall by 40% between weeks 1 and 4 for new cohorts. Walk through how you would diagnose and respond.

title: Design a product for small landlords screening tenants
prompt: Design a product that helps independent landlords (1-5 units) screen rental applicants fairly and quickly. What is the riskiest assumption, and how would you de-risk it?

title: Onboard switchers from a competitor
prompt: A productivity app sees a wave of users coming from a competitor that just had a price hike. How would you redesign onboarding for this segment?

title: Should a fitness app remove its free tier?
prompt: A fitness app considers removing its free tier to push paid conversion. What signals would you look at, and how would you make the call?

title: Telehealth scheduling drop-off
prompt: A telehealth product sees 40% of patients drop off at the scheduling step. How would you investigate, and what is the first thing you would test?

title: Design a college-application companion for high schoolers
prompt: Design a product that helps US high-school juniors and seniors plan their college applications. Where are the biggest gaps in current tools, and what would you ship first?

title: Reduce no-shows for at-home services
prompt: An at-home services marketplace sees 12% of bookings result in no-shows by the customer. How would you reduce that without hurting booking volume?

title: Design a product for nurses changing hospitals
prompt: Design a product that helps nurses move between hospitals smoothly — credentials, scheduling, onboarding. Who is the riskiest user and what would you ship in the first three months?

title: Design a product for households tracking shared finances
prompt: Design a product for households who share finances across multiple earners. What is the wedge segment, what is the smallest valuable v1, and how would you measure success at month three?

title: Design invoicing for freelancers
prompt: Design a product that helps freelancers send invoices, chase payment, and reconcile across clients. Who is the riskiest segment, and what would you build first to earn their trust?

title: Design a tool for renters disputing repairs
prompt: Design a product that helps renters document and dispute repair issues with landlords. Where is the hardest workflow, and how would you protect both sides from abuse?

title: Design investor updates for solo founders
prompt: Design a product that helps solo founders write and send investor updates without it eating a Sunday. What is the smallest feature set that wins their first three months of usage?

title: Help retirees stay socially connected
prompt: Design a product that helps retired adults maintain in-person and remote social ties. Where is the loneliest gap, and what does week-one engagement look like?

title: Design for first-generation college applicants
prompt: Design a product for first-generation US college applicants who lack a counselor at home. Where is the highest-leverage moment in their journey, and what would you ship for it first?

title: Recovery tracking for amateur athletes
prompt: Design a product for amateur athletes (runners, cyclists, lifters) tracking recovery and injury risk. Who is the wedge user and what one signal would you build the v1 around?

title: Bookkeeping for owner-operators
prompt: Design a bookkeeping product for small-business owners who do not have an accountant. What is the riskiest assumption about user behaviour, and how would you de-risk it before building?

title: Coordinate childcare across households
prompt: Design a product that helps separated or co-parenting families coordinate childcare schedules, expenses, and handoffs. Where would adoption stall, and what would you build first to get past it?

title: Onboarding for prosumer creators
prompt: Design the onboarding for a prosumer creator tool (think podcast or video) where mastery is the moat. What is the activation moment, and how do you get a brand-new user there in 10 minutes?

title: Design a climate impact tracker for households
prompt: Design a climate-impact tracker for households who want to act but do not know where to start. What is the smallest behaviour you can change in 30 days, and how do you measure it honestly?

title: Design financial literacy for teens
prompt: Design a product that teaches financial literacy to 13-17 year olds. Who is the buyer vs. the user, and how do you balance parental trust with teen autonomy?

title: Cut cart abandonment on luxury e-commerce
prompt: A luxury e-commerce site has a 78% cart-abandonment rate, well above the 65% category average. Where would you investigate first, and what would you ship to lift conversion 5 percentage points?

title: Improve creator-to-fan response rates
prompt: On a UGC platform, creators reply to less than 10% of fan messages. How would you raise reply rates without increasing creator burnout?

title: Reduce time-to-first-deploy for a dev tool
prompt: A developer tool sees a median time-to-first-deploy of 47 minutes for new accounts. How would you cut that in half without removing capability?

title: Search relevance for a niche video catalog
prompt: A niche video product (cooking tutorials, lap-by-lap motorsports, etc.) has weak search relevance: 22% of sessions click no result. How would you improve relevance for a small but loyal user base?

title: Reduce ticket re-opens on a B2B product
prompt: On a B2B support product, 18% of tickets are re-opened within seven days. What does that signal, and how would you address it without rewarding agents to close tickets prematurely?

title: Lift trial-to-paid for a productivity SaaS
prompt: A productivity SaaS converts 3.2% of trial users to paid; the leadership target is 6%. What would you investigate, and what is the first experiment you would run?

title: Improve listing quality on a peer-to-peer rental
prompt: A peer-to-peer rental marketplace finds 35% of listings have fewer than three photos and convert at half the rate. How would you raise listing quality without alienating casual hosts?

title: Lift review-completion on a hospitality marketplace
prompt: On a hospitality marketplace, only 41% of guests leave a review after a stay. Reviews are the trust currency. How would you raise that to 60%?

title: Diagnose drop-off at 'import contacts'
prompt: On a social product, 31% of new users drop off at the 'import contacts' step. What hypotheses would you test, and what would you ship to lift completion without sacrificing trust?

title: Diagnose week-2 churn for habit tracking
prompt: A habit-tracking app sees 55% of week-1 actives become inactive by week 2. Walk through how you would diagnose, and which segment you would protect first.

title: SMB churn on a B2B product
prompt: A B2B SaaS sees SMB customers churn at 2x the rate of mid-market. Where would you investigate, and how would you decide between fixing the product vs. fixing acquisition?

title: Identity-verification drop-off
prompt: On a fintech onboarding, 18% of users abandon at 'verify identity'. How would you investigate the cause, and what would you ship first to lift completion without weakening compliance?

title: Country-specific checkout drop
prompt: Your checkout converts at 8% in country A but only 3% in country B with similar traffic. How would you diagnose the gap, and what would you ship if it is mostly payment-method coverage?

title: Drop-off on slow networks
prompt: Telemetry shows users on sub-3G networks drop off 3x more often during onboarding. How do you decide whether to invest in performance, fallback flows, or segment exclusion?

title: Wedge segment for a calendar product
prompt: You are launching a new calendar product. Pick the wedge segment, defend the choice, and describe what you would deliberately not do for the first six months.

title: Underserved segment for an LMS
prompt: Pick an underserved segment for a corporate learning product (LMS) and design the v1 around them. Explain why incumbents have left this segment behind.

title: Underserved segment for code review
prompt: Pick an underserved segment for a code-review product (think small open-source teams, security teams, agencies) and design the v1. What is your moat at year three?

title: Two segments, one roadmap
prompt: A meditation app could double down on stressed professionals or new parents. You can only pick one for the next two quarters. Walk through the call and what changes about the product.

title: Pick the next country for a fintech
prompt: A consumer fintech is in three countries and wants to add a fourth. How would you choose, and what is the riskiest assumption your decision rests on?

title: Bootstrap supply on a niche labor marketplace
prompt: You are bootstrapping the supply side of a niche labor marketplace (think on-demand legal review, multilingual tutoring). What is your supply strategy for the first 90 days?

title: Reduce off-platform leakage on a freelance marketplace
prompt: On a freelance marketplace, you suspect 20% of repeat work is happening off-platform after the first job. How would you measure it, and how would you address it without driving freelancers away?

title: Match quality on a tutoring marketplace
prompt: On a tutoring marketplace, only 28% of first sessions lead to a second booking. How would you improve match quality without slowing down the booking flow?

title: Address fraudulent listings on a peer-to-peer rental
prompt: Trust and safety reports a rising rate of fraudulent listings on a peer-to-peer rental product. How would you balance speed of removal, host appeal rights, and platform liability?

title: Make a banking app usable for low-vision users
prompt: A consumer banking app has not been audited for low-vision users. Walk through your plan: what to fix first, how to validate, and how to bake accessibility into the team's process going forward.

title: Onboard users with limited tech literacy
prompt: A consumer product is expanding into segments with limited prior smartphone experience. How would you redesign onboarding without dumbing the product down for everyone else?

title: Adapt for low-bandwidth regions
prompt: Your consumer app is launching in regions where 60% of users have <2 Mbps mobile bandwidth. How would you adapt the product, and what would you measure to know it worked?

title: Design an AI-native travel planner
prompt: Design an AI-native travel planning product. Where does the AI add real leverage vs. existing search/aggregator products, and what would you absolutely not let it do at v1?

title: AI tutor for adult language learners
prompt: Design an AI tutor for adults learning a second language. How would you measure pedagogy quality, and what is your fallback when the model is uncertain?

title: AI-native CRM for solo founders
prompt: Design an AI-native CRM for solo founders who hate CRMs. What does the AI do that the user does not have to, and what is the trust contract?

title: Add AI to meal planning without breaking trust
prompt: A meal-planning app wants to add AI generation. Trust is fragile (allergies, dietary restrictions). How would you stage the rollout, and what is your launch bar?

title: Add paid plans to a free-only consumer app
prompt: A free-only consumer app with 4M MAU wants to introduce paid plans. How would you choose what to lock behind a paywall without alienating the existing base?

title: Ads vs. subscription for a content product
prompt: A content product is considering subscription, ads, or a hybrid. Frame the decision and pick one — including what you would change about the product if you went subscription-only.

title: Choose metrics for a creator marketplace
prompt: You own a creator marketplace where brands hire creators for short campaigns. Define the North Star metric, guardrail metrics, and the first dashboard you would build.

title: Diagnose a 12-point activation drop
prompt: Activation just fell from 64% to 52% week-over-week with no known launch. Walk through your diagnostic playbook in priority order.

title: North Star for a B2B collaboration tool
prompt: Pick a North Star metric for a B2B collaboration tool that wants to deepen team usage, not just seat count. Justify it and name the input metrics.

title: North Star for a music free tier
prompt: Define a North Star and counter-metrics for the free tier of a music-streaming product whose explicit goal is to convert free users to paid.

title: A/B test a checkout redesign
prompt: Design an A/B test for a major checkout redesign. What are your primary, secondary, and guardrail metrics, and how would you decide significance?

title: Conversion up, session length down
prompt: An experiment shows +3% checkout conversion but -1% session length. Would you ship it, and what would you check before deciding?

title: Onboarding completion vs. day-7 retention
prompt: An onboarding change raises completion by 15% but drops day-7 retention by 4%. How do you frame the trade-off and decide?

title: Two segments, opposite signals
prompt: An experiment looks neutral overall but power users react positively while new users react negatively. What would you do next?

title: Engagement drops a week after launch
prompt: Engagement drops 6% the week after a feature launches, but the launch was small. How would you separate signal from noise and decide on rollback?

title: Retention up, ARPU down
prompt: Retention is up 5% over a quarter but average revenue per user is down 7% in the same window. How do you reason about it and what do you do?

title: Input metrics for a creator's first 14 days
prompt: You want creators on a UGC platform to reach 'activated' state in 14 days. Choose the input metrics you would track and why.

title: Power and runtime for an A/B test
prompt: Your team wants to detect a 1.5% lift in checkout conversion. Walk through how you would estimate the required sample size and runtime.

title: Drop-off at 'add payment'
prompt: On a subscription product, 38% of users abandon at the 'add payment' step. How would you investigate, and what would you ship first to lift conversion?

title: North Star for a podcast platform
prompt: Pick a North Star metric for a podcast platform that wants to grow listening, not just sign-ups. Justify the choice and name two input metrics that move it.

title: North Star for a job-search product
prompt: Pick a North Star metric for a job-search product. The CEO says 'applications submitted'; argue for or against, and propose what you would actually use.

title: North Star for a clinic EHR
prompt: Pick a North Star for an electronic health-records product used by small clinics. Patient outcomes, clinician hours saved, and visits charted are all candidates — pick one and defend it.

title: North Star for a school-district product
prompt: Pick a North Star metric for a product sold to K-12 school districts. Parents, teachers, and admins all matter — whose value do you anchor on, and why?

title: Rewriting a North Star at year five
prompt: Your product is five years old. The current North Star (DAU) feels stale and is gaming itself. Walk through how you would propose a replacement to the executive team and migrate org behaviour.

title: Input metrics for trial-to-paid
prompt: Trial-to-paid is your output metric. Pick three input metrics that meaningfully move it and explain how you would track each weekly without drowning the team in dashboards.

title: Input metrics for week-1 retention
prompt: Week-1 retention is the metric your team commits to next quarter. Pick the input metrics you would actually instrument and ship, and the one you would deliberately not chase.

title: Input metrics for content quality
prompt: On a UGC product, leadership says 'we need higher content quality'. Translate that into 2-3 input metrics that engineers can actually move, and a counter-metric to keep the team honest.

title: Input metrics for marketplace liquidity
prompt: On a two-sided marketplace, define liquidity precisely and pick the input metrics that drive it. How would you trade off supply growth vs. demand growth in a quarter where both lag?

title: Pricing-page bounce
prompt: Your pricing page has a 72% bounce rate. The PM intuition is that the page is confusing. How would you actually validate that, and what is the smallest change worth running first?

title: Sign-up → first action funnel
prompt: You own a B2B onboarding funnel: sign-up, invite, first action, first value. Walk through how you would diagnose a 30% drop between 'invite' and 'first action'.

title: Drop-off at 'invite teammates'
prompt: On a collaboration product, 80% of new users skip the 'invite teammates' step. Should you make it required, redesign it, or accept the drop-off? Make a defensible call.

title: First A/B test on a small product
prompt: Your product gets 5,000 weekly visitors. You want to A/B test a new home page. What is realistic to test at this scale, and what is not? Defend your runtime and metric choices.

title: Segment a small experiment
prompt: An experiment is too small to detect a 1% lift overall but you suspect power users react differently. Should you slice it? When does that help, and when is it just noise?

title: Decide significance with a weak signal
prompt: Your A/B shows +0.4% conversion at p=0.18 after the planned runtime. Leadership wants a decision. How do you frame the call, and what additional information would you ask for?

title: Long-running test got bumped
prompt: Your A/B test was running for two weeks when an emergency hotfix changed the pricing page. Is the test salvageable? Walk through how you would assess the damage and decide.

title: Multi-cell test with four variants
prompt: You want to test four variants of an onboarding flow simultaneously. What are the trade-offs of running A/B/C/D vs. sequential A/Bs? Pick one and defend it for a 200K-MAU product.

title: Interpreting a flat A/B
prompt: Your A/B came back flat across primary, secondary, and guardrail metrics. Is the experiment a failure, a non-event, or a quiet success? How do you communicate the result?

title: Opposite-direction segments
prompt: An A/B is +5% for new users and -3% for returning users, statistically significant in both. Walk through how you would decide whether to ship, ramp, or kill.

title: Primary up, guardrail down
prompt: Primary metric is +2.4%, guardrail (refunds) is +0.3pp. The PM lead wants to ship. The data lead wants to wait. Frame the call and recommend.

title: Suspecting a seasonality artifact
prompt: Your A/B started the week of a holiday and shows a 4% lift. Could it be real? Walk through how you would check for a seasonality artifact, and what you would do about it.

title: Choose a minimum detectable effect
prompt: Your team is debating MDE for a redesign experiment: 1%, 2%, or 4%. Frame the trade-off (runtime vs. risk vs. learning) and recommend a defensible choice for a 1M-MAU consumer app.

title: Pre/post launch causal claim
prompt: A team launched a feature without an A/B and now claims credit for a 3% retention lift. Walk through how you would assess whether the claim is causal, correlated, or coincidental.

title: Diff-in-diff for a regional rollout
prompt: Your product launched a feature in two markets but not three others. Sketch a diff-in-differences analysis to estimate the causal impact, including the assumptions and where they break.

title: Read a 4-week retention curve
prompt: Your week-1, week-2, week-3, week-4 retention is 60/45/40/38 for the latest cohort. Is that a healthy curve or not? What signals what, and what would you compare it against?

title: Paid vs. organic cohort divergence
prompt: Paid cohorts retain at 55% week-4; organic cohorts retain at 75%. Both are growing in volume. What does the gap tell you, and what would you change?

title: Define and track power users
prompt: Your CEO asks for the 'power user' metric for a productivity tool. Define it precisely, justify the threshold, and explain how you would track its share of the active base over time.

title: Search speed vs. result accuracy
prompt: Reducing search latency by 200ms is feasible but costs 1.5pp of result-relevance score. Frame the trade-off for a consumer search product and recommend.

title: Engagement vs. wellbeing
prompt: A consumer social app is debating whether to optimize for time-spent or user-reported wellbeing. The two correlate negatively in your current design. Walk through how you would frame and decide.

title: Short-term vs. long-term metrics
prompt: A growth team's launch is +6% sign-ups but suspected -2% 90-day retention. Which metric wins, and how do you keep an org from gaming the short-term one for the next four quarters?

title: Paid conversion vs. trust
prompt: Adding a more aggressive upsell modal lifts paid conversion 8% but drops NPS 4 points. Make a defensible call and explain how you would unwind it if you were wrong.

title: DAU jumped 8%, suspicious
prompt: DAU is up 8% week-over-week with no launches. Your gut says it is bot traffic or instrumentation, not real growth. How would you confirm or deny in two days?

title: Paid traffic conversion fell
prompt: Paid-traffic conversion fell 12% week-over-week; organic is unchanged. Walk through how you would diagnose, and how you would split the work with the marketing team.

title: Search CTR dropped
prompt: Search CTR fell from 28% to 24% over a month. Is it a quality issue, a UI issue, an index issue, or a mix? How would you decompose and respond?

title: Premium plan churn spiked
prompt: Your Premium plan saw monthly churn jump from 3.2% to 4.7% in the latest period. Where would you investigate, and what is the smallest action that could move the needle in 30 days?

title: Weekly business review for an app
prompt: Design the weekly business review dashboard for a consumer app PM team. What goes on it, what does not, and how do you keep it from becoming wallpaper?

title: Exec dashboard for an AI product
prompt: Design an executive dashboard for an AI product where quality, latency, and cost all matter. What three numbers does the CEO see, and how do you avoid Goodhart on each?

title: Operational dashboard for a marketplace
prompt: Design the day-to-day operational dashboard for a two-sided marketplace ops team. What signals an actionable problem, and what is just background noise?

title: Anomaly alerts for sign-ups
prompt: Define an alerting rule for unusual changes in daily sign-ups. What threshold catches real problems without paging the PM at 3am twice a week?

title: Multi-touch attribution debate
prompt: Marketing wants last-click attribution; finance wants first-click; product wants something in the middle. Frame the debate and recommend a model for a SaaS with a 30-day trial.

title: Pick the first events to instrument
prompt: You are PM on a brand-new product. The team can instrument 10 events for v1. Which 10, and how do you avoid event-naming chaos at month six?

title: Find usage clusters in the data
prompt: Data science finds three distinct usage clusters in your product. How would you decide which one is signal worth designing for vs. an artifact of how the data is sliced?

title: Design evals for an AI support assistant
prompt: Your company wants to launch an AI support assistant that can answer billing questions. How would you decide whether it is safe and useful enough to launch?

title: Eval framework for an AI code-review assistant
prompt: Design the eval suite for an AI code-review assistant that suggests inline fixes on pull requests. What dimensions would you score, and what is your launch bar?

title: When is human-in-the-loop required?
prompt: An AI scheduling agent can propose, modify, and confirm meetings on a user's behalf. Where would you require human-in-the-loop, and how would you decide?

title: Recovering from a viral bad answer
prompt: Your AI product gives a bad answer that goes viral. How do you recover trust with users, regulators, and internal stakeholders, in that order?

title: Latency vs. cost vs. quality on AI chat
prompt: You own an AI chat product. Walk through how you would balance latency, cost, and quality across model choices and routing. What does your dashboard look like?

title: Fallback when the model is uncertain
prompt: Design the fallback behaviour for an AI feature when the model is uncertain. What is the user experience, what data do you collect, and what does success look like?

title: Should a startup fine-tune on customer data?
prompt: A startup is debating whether to fine-tune a model on its enterprise customers' data. What would you align on with legal, security, and engineering before deciding?

title: A new jailbreak goes viral
prompt: A new jailbreak technique against your AI assistant goes viral. What are the first three actions you take in the next 24 hours, and what is your 30-day plan?

title: Launch criteria for an AI sales assistant
prompt: Define the launch criteria for an AI sales assistant in a regulated industry (e.g., financial services). What is on the bar, and what would block a launch?

title: Evals for an AI K-12 math tutor
prompt: Design evals for an AI tutor for K-12 math. How do you measure correctness, pedagogy quality, and safety? What is the bar for general availability?

title: Third-party model vs. self-host
prompt: Your team is choosing between a third-party frontier model and self-hosting an open-weights model. Frame the decision and recommend an approach.

title: Tell me about a disagreement with engineering
prompt: Tell me about a time you disagreed with engineering or design on what to build. What happened and what did you learn?

title: Influence without authority
prompt: Tell me about a time you drove a meaningful outcome without formal authority. Walk through the situation, your moves, and the measurable result.

title: Biggest failure as a PM
prompt: What is the biggest failure of your PM career, and what did you change as a result? Be specific about what was hard.

title: A time data changed your mind
prompt: Tell me about a time data changed your mind on a meaningful product decision. What did you originally believe, and what convinced you?

title: Prioritizing a roadmap with too many asks
prompt: Walk me through the hardest prioritization call you have made on a roadmap. How did you frame it, and how did you defend the cuts?

title: Conflict with a senior leader
prompt: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a senior leader on a product decision. What did you do, and how did it resolve?

title: Pushed back on an executive request
prompt: Describe a time you pushed back on an executive ask that you believed was wrong. How did you handle it, and what was the outcome?

title: A hard deadline you missed
prompt: Tell me about a hard deadline you missed. How did you communicate it, and what changed afterward?

title: Coached a struggling teammate
prompt: Tell me about a time you helped a teammate who was struggling. What did you actually do week by week, and what was the outcome?

title: Most measurable impact
prompt: Tell me about the project where you delivered the most measurable impact. Quantify the outcome and your specific contribution.

title: Raising an ethical concern
prompt: Tell me about a time you raised an ethical concern about a product decision internally. How did you raise it, and what happened?

title: Should a B2B SaaS product add an AI copilot?
prompt: A mature B2B SaaS product wants to add an AI copilot because competitors are doing it. How would you decide whether to invest?

title: Build, buy, or partner: AI photo editor
prompt: Your consumer app wants an AI-powered photo editor. Should you build, buy, or partner? Frame the decision and pick a path.

title: Pricing for a freemium developer tool
prompt: Recommend a pricing structure for a freemium developer tool with strong individual usage but weak team monetization. What would you change first?

title: Reposition against an upmarket competitor
prompt: A low-end CRM faces a stronger upmarket competitor moving down. How would you reposition the product, and what would you give up?

title: Bundle or charge separately for AI?
prompt: Should a productivity SaaS bundle a new AI feature into the existing plans or sell it as a paid add-on? Make the call and defend it.

title: Move down to SMB or stay enterprise?
prompt: An enterprise SaaS sees growth slowing. The CEO asks if you should open an SMB tier. How do you analyze and recommend?

title: Pivot from feature to platform
prompt: A successful single-feature product wants to become a platform. When is the right time to pivot, and what would you ship in the first 90 days?

title: Cold-start strategy for a marketplace
prompt: You are seeding a two-sided marketplace from zero in a single city. Walk through your supply-side and demand-side strategy for the first 6 months.

title: Moat for an AI-native note-taking app
prompt: What is the durable advantage of an AI-native note-taking app three years out? What would you invest in now to make that real?

title: Plan a partner API launch
prompt: Your product needs to expose a partner API for enterprise customers. What would you align on with engineering before launch?

title: Postmortem you led
prompt: Walk me through a production incident postmortem you led. What was the failure mode, how did you communicate, and what changed structurally afterward?

title: Microservices vs. monolith for a small team
prompt: A 12-engineer team is debating microservices vs. monolith for a new product. What is your role as PM in that conversation, and how do you frame the trade-off?

title: Batch vs. streaming for a metrics product
prompt: Your team is deciding between a batch and streaming pipeline for a real-time metrics product. Frame the trade-off and recommend an approach.

title: Rate limits for a public API
prompt: Design rate limits for a public partner API. What are the tiers, the headers, and the failure modes you would document?

title: Migrate to a new identity provider
prompt: Your product has to migrate to a new identity provider in 90 days. Walk through your plan with engineering, security, and customer success.

title: SLOs for a payments product
prompt: Define SLOs for a payments product. Which user journeys do you cover, what windows do you choose, and how do you tie them to the on-call rotation?

title: Daily ride volume in a mid-size city
prompt: Estimate the daily ride-share volume in a mid-size US city. State your assumptions clearly and pressure-test the largest one.

title: Streaming revenue in a country
prompt: Estimate the annual revenue of a major streaming service in a country of 50M people. Walk through your structure.

title: Prioritize 5 roadmap items
prompt: You have 5 roadmap items with mixed effort and impact and a quarter to ship. Walk through how you would choose and how you would defend it to leadership.

title: Search query volume in a region
prompt: Estimate daily search query volume for a major search engine in a region of your choice. State assumptions and identify which one is most uncertain.

title: Bug fixes vs. features in a 2-week sprint
prompt: You have a 2-week sprint, an aging bug backlog, and a feature commitment to leadership. Walk through how you would split the team and the trade-off.

title: Storage cost for a cloud photo product
prompt: Estimate the monthly storage cost for a cloud photo product with 10M monthly active users. What does the unit economics look like at scale?

title: Built consensus across three disagreeing teams
prompt: Tell me about a time you got three teams who disagreed to commit to a shared plan. What was the disagreement, what did you actually do, and what was the outcome?

title: Convinced engineering on a controversial direction
prompt: Tell me about a time you got engineering on board with a direction they initially resisted. What was the resistance, and how did you change it without forcing the call?

title: Took ownership in ambiguity
prompt: Tell me about a time you took ownership of a problem nobody else owned. How did you decide it was yours to take, and what changed because you took it?

title: Drove a cross-functional plan with no mandate
prompt: Describe a project you drove across functions without formal authority. What did you have to do that a senior leader would not have had to, and what did the work cost you?

title: Held direction through shifting priorities
prompt: Tell me about a quarter where leadership changed priorities mid-cycle. How did you protect the team's focus while still being responsive to the change?

title: Conflict with a co-PM on overlapping scope
prompt: Tell me about a conflict with a peer PM whose scope overlapped yours. What was the friction, what did you do about it, and how did the relationship end up?

title: Disagreement with a designer on UX direction
prompt: Tell me about a substantive design disagreement with a designer you respected. What was the disagreement, what convinced you (or them), and how did the user benefit?

title: Pushed back on a legal or compliance ask
prompt: Tell me about a time legal or compliance asked for something that would meaningfully hurt the user experience. How did you push back, and what did you learn about the partnership?

title: Disputed an engineering estimate
prompt: Tell me about a time you disputed an engineering estimate that felt off. How did you approach it without damaging trust, and what was the actual outcome?

title: Disagreement with a peer PM on prioritization
prompt: Describe a prioritization disagreement with a peer PM where both of you had a defensible case. How did you actually resolve it, and what would you do differently next time?

title: Worked with a passive-aggressive teammate
prompt: Tell me about a teammate whose behaviour was undermining the team. How did you address it, and what did you learn about your own response under that kind of pressure?

title: A launch that failed
prompt: Tell me about a launch that did not deliver the impact you projected. What did you genuinely get wrong, and what changed in how you operate as a result?

title: A hire that did not work out
prompt: Tell me about a hire (PM, designer, engineer) you advocated for who did not work out. What signals did you miss, and what is your hiring bar now because of it?

title: A decision you reversed publicly
prompt: Describe a public reversal — a decision you committed to and then publicly walked back. How did you communicate it, and what did you do to keep the team's trust?

title: Talked directly to a frustrated customer
prompt: Tell me about a time you spoke directly with a frustrated customer about a product issue. What did you learn, and how did it change what your team built next?

title: Built for a niche segment over loud asks
prompt: Tell me about a time you built for a niche segment of users when louder voices wanted something else. How did you defend the choice, and what was the outcome?

title: Killed a feature based on user research
prompt: Tell me about a feature you championed and then killed because of what users actually showed you. What did you learn, and what did you tell the team that built it?

title: Said no to a customer ask
prompt: Tell me about a time you said no to a customer who really wanted something. How did you frame the no, and how did the relationship hold up?

title: Worked through a re-org
prompt: Tell me about a re-org that affected your team. How did you keep the team's focus, and what did you actually do in the first two weeks after the announcement?

title: Ramped into a new team fast
prompt: Describe how you ramped into a new team in your first 30 days. What were your priorities by week, and what did you deliberately not do?

title: Took over a legacy product
prompt: Tell me about a time you took over a product that had been on autopilot. What did you do in the first 90 days, and what was the hardest part?

title: Partnered with sales or marketing
prompt: Tell me about a deep partnership you built with sales or marketing for a launch. What did each of you commit to, and how did you handle when one side slipped?

title: Communicated bad news to leadership
prompt: Tell me about a time you had to deliver bad news to executive leadership. How did you prepare, what did you say first, and what was the outcome?

title: Wrote a strategy doc that landed
prompt: Walk me through a strategy doc you wrote that actually changed direction. What was the moment you knew it had landed, and what would you write differently now?

title: Gave a presentation that did not land
prompt: Tell me about a presentation that did not land. What was the room reaction, what did you do in the moment, and what did you change for the next one?

title: High-stakes call with limited data
prompt: Describe a high-stakes call you made with limited data — a launch decision, a hire, a kill. How did you frame the risk, and how did it turn out?

title: Time you over-invested
prompt: Tell me about a project you over-invested in. What were the signals you missed, and how do you spot them earlier today?

title: Time you under-invested and regretted it
prompt: Describe a project you treated as low-priority that turned out to matter. What were the early signals you ignored, and what did you change about your prioritization process?

title: Annual coffee consumption in NYC
prompt: Estimate the total cups of coffee consumed per day in New York City. State your assumptions clearly and identify which one your final number is most sensitive to.

title: Gym memberships in a US state
prompt: Estimate the number of active gym memberships in a US state of around 6 million people. Walk through the structure, and explain how you would pressure-test the largest assumption.

title: Daily Uber rides in San Francisco
prompt: Estimate the number of ride-share rides taken in San Francisco on a typical weekday. State assumptions and your decomposition before you commit to a number.

title: Annual flights from a major hub
prompt: Estimate the number of passenger flights leaving a major international airport (e.g., LAX, LHR) per year. Walk through your structure and identify the biggest source of error.

title: Holiday-day SMS volume
prompt: Estimate the total number of text messages sent in the US on a single major holiday (e.g., New Year's Eve). State your structure and what would change your estimate by 2x.

title: Ad revenue per hour of TV
prompt: Estimate the ad revenue per primetime hour of a top US broadcast network. Lay out the structure: viewers, ad load, CPM, and how you would sanity-check the result.

title: Bandwidth cost of video calls
prompt: Estimate the monthly bandwidth cost for a video-call product with 5M monthly actives spending 2 hours/week on calls. Walk through assumptions and where the unit economics break.

title: Compute cost for an AI feature
prompt: Estimate the monthly compute cost for an AI summary feature on a 10M-MAU product, where 30% of users use it weekly. State your assumptions about token cost, length, and routing.

title: Database read volume for a social feed
prompt: Estimate daily read queries for a social feed serving 50M MAU with 20 minutes/day of usage. State your structure and the assumption that would 5x the result.

title: Three customers, one ask
prompt: Three of your top-10 enterprise customers ask for the same feature in the same week. How do you decide whether to commit, defer, or build a workaround?

title: Tech debt vs. launch deadline
prompt: Engineering wants two weeks for tech debt. Sales wants a feature launched in three. You can have one. Walk through how you decide and how you communicate the call.

title: Board member overrides your roadmap
prompt: A board member personally requests a feature that displaces your top priority. How do you handle the moment, the CEO, and the team — without making it political?

title: Three squads, shared dependency
prompt: Three squads need the same platform team for the next quarter. The platform team has bandwidth for one. How do you facilitate the prioritization across PMs without becoming a bottleneck?

title: Pick the next quarter for an early-stage team
prompt: You are 6 weeks into leading a 3-person early-stage team. Pick the priority for the next quarter from a list of five plausible directions and defend it.

title: Trim a 10-item backlog to 3
prompt: Your team has a 10-item backlog and capacity for 3 items this sprint. Walk through the framework you use to trim, and how you communicate the cut to stakeholders.

title: Effort for a new analytics dashboard
prompt: Estimate the realistic build effort for a new analytics dashboard surfacing five KPIs across three roles. State assumptions about data availability, design, and review cycles.

title: Effort for a multi-region launch
prompt: Estimate the build and launch effort to go from a single-region product to a three-region launch (North America, Europe, APAC). What is the riskiest line item?

title: Eval framework for an AI search product
prompt: Design the eval suite for an AI search product (think AI-native answers, not classic ranking). What dimensions do you score, what is your golden set, and what is the launch bar?

title: Evaluate AI-generated UI components
prompt: An AI feature generates working UI components for a no-code product. How would you evaluate quality across correctness, taste, and accessibility? Where would you not let it ship without human review?

title: First-time-user trust for AI
prompt: What is the first-time-user trust pattern for an AI feature on an established product? Walk through the in-product moments and what each one is doing.

title: Fallback for AI image generation
prompt: Your AI image generation feature fails to produce a usable image about 18% of the time. Design the fallback experience without making the failure rate the user's problem.

title: Undo for an AI agent action
prompt: Your AI agent can take real actions (send emails, schedule meetings). Design the undo and confirm experience. What is reversible, what is not, and how does the user know which is which?

title: Smart routing between AI models
prompt: Your product is paying $0.18 per AI request on the frontier model. You want to route easy queries to a cheaper model. Walk through how you would design the router and the eval to validate it.

title: Caching strategy for an AI assistant
prompt: Design a caching strategy for an AI assistant where 30% of queries are near-duplicates. What do you cache, for how long, and what are the privacy and freshness implications?

title: Lower quality to hit a cost bar
prompt: Margins on an AI feature are negative at scale. The CFO wants a 30% cost cut next quarter. Where do you propose to lower quality, and what would you refuse to compromise on?

title: Hallucinations in a legal AI
prompt: An AI assistant for legal research occasionally cites cases that do not exist. What is your detection plan, your in-product mitigation, and what the user is told?

title: Signal uncertainty to the user
prompt: Design how an AI feature signals uncertainty to the user without crying wolf. What language, UI, and threshold do you choose, and how do you measure if it works?

title: Label vs. watermark AI output
prompt: A regulator wants AI-generated content labeled. Walk through the trade-offs of in-product labels, metadata watermarks, and visible watermarks. What would you recommend for a consumer-facing product?

title: Use synthetic data to train
prompt: A team proposes training on AI-generated synthetic data to fix a known coverage gap. Walk through the case for and against, and what guardrails you would require before approving.

title: Design a beta for an AI feature
prompt: Design the beta program for an AI feature that you expect to need 6-8 weeks of real-user evaluation before GA. Who is in, what they get, what you measure, and what graduates the feature.

title: Sunset an old AI model version
prompt: Your product has been on model v1 for a year and v2 is meaningfully better but slightly different. Design the migration: cutover plan, comms, safety net, and rollback criteria.

title: Edge cases in AI moderation
prompt: An AI moderation system is wrong on 0.5% of cases. The errors include false bans of long-time users. Design the appeals path, the dashboard, and the rule for when to retrain.

title: Onboard users to an AI feature
prompt: An established product is rolling out an AI feature to 200M existing users. Design the onboarding moment: how to show the value, how to set the trust contract, and how to opt out.

title: Versioning a public API
prompt: Design a versioning strategy for a public API consumed by third-party developers. URL versioning, header versioning, or evergreen with deprecation? Make the call and explain the trade-off.

title: Communicate a breaking API change
prompt: You need to ship a breaking change in a public API used by 800 partners. Walk through the deprecation timeline, the comms plan, and what you would commit to in writing.

title: GraphQL vs REST for a partner API
prompt: A new partner API can be GraphQL or REST. Walk through the trade-offs for partner DX, internal eng cost, and observability, and recommend a path.

title: Internal-only API or partner-grade?
prompt: Engineering wants to ship an API as internal-only. Sales wants the same shape exposed to partners. What is the real cost of going partner-grade, and how do you decide?

title: Webhooks vs polling design
prompt: Partners want real-time updates on events in your platform. Walk through the trade-offs of webhooks, long-polling, and server-sent events, and pick a design.

title: Pagination semantics
prompt: Pick a pagination approach (offset, cursor, keyset) for a public API that returns large result sets. Explain the trade-offs for partners and for your backend.

title: Re-platforming roadmap
prompt: Engineering wants to re-platform the core service over the next 4 quarters. Sales has a $2M deal that needs the legacy stack. Walk through how you would build the joint plan.

title: When to require a tech spec
prompt: Define the rule for when an engineering project requires a written tech spec on your team. Where is the bar low, where is it high, and how do you keep the process from slowing the team down?

title: Quarterly tech-debt budget
prompt: Engineering wants 25% of capacity for tech debt. Product wants 5%. Walk through how you would land the conversation, and what data you would bring to the meeting.

title: Cross-team architecture review
prompt: Two teams are proposing incompatible architectures for a shared dependency. As PM, what is your role in the review, and how do you keep it from becoming a turf war?

title: Scope a 1-week spike
prompt: Engineering proposes a 1-week spike on a hard problem (e.g., does a new approach work for our scale?). Define the success criteria for the spike and what gets decided at the end.

title: Set an error budget
prompt: Define an error budget for a product where reliability really matters (payments, healthcare, education). Walk through how you set the budget and what changes when it is exhausted.

title: Build a postmortem culture
prompt: Your team has a habit of skipping postmortems for non-customer-facing incidents. Walk through how you would change that, and what you would commit to as PM in each one.

title: Capacity planning for a launch
prompt: Marketing is planning a campaign that will spike traffic 4x for a week. Walk through the capacity-planning conversation with engineering, and what your decision rule is if costs blow up.

title: Privacy-aware analytics design
prompt: Your analytics stack is collecting more than you need. Walk through how you would design a privacy-aware analytics plan that still answers the questions PM and growth need.

title: Migrate off a vendor dependency
prompt: You depend on a vendor whose pricing just doubled. Walk through the build-vs-stay-vs-switch decision over the next two quarters, and how you would communicate it to the team and customers.

title: Acquire a community or build it
prompt: Your product would benefit from an active developer community. Should you acquire an existing one (acqui-hire its team) or grow your own? Walk through the cases.

title: Open-source a developer tool
prompt: You own a profitable closed-source developer tool. A scrappy open-source competitor is gaining usage. Should you open-source the core, and what changes about your business if you do?

title: Build a platform from a single feature
prompt: A single feature of your product is generating outsized engagement. The CEO wants to make it a platform. What is the case for that, and what is the case for keeping it a feature?

title: Sequence international expansion
prompt: You have two viable next markets — one with high revenue per user but slow growth, one with lower revenue but a fast-growing user base. Sequence them and defend the call.

title: Vertical-specific product variant
prompt: Your horizontal SaaS could ship a vertical variant for healthcare. Walk through the strategic case for and against, and what would have to be true to commit.

title: Unbundle a feature into a separate product
prompt: One feature of your product is increasingly used standalone. Should you unbundle it as a separate paid product? Walk through the cannibalization, distribution, and pricing implications.

title: Loyalty program for a SaaS
prompt: A consumer loyalty program is a familiar pattern. Would it work for a SaaS, and if so what would it look like? Make a defensible recommendation for a B2B product of your choice.

title: Monetize a free distribution channel
prompt: Your product has a popular free distribution channel (a chrome extension, a mobile widget, a community Slack). The CFO wants it monetized. How do you do that without killing the channel?

title: Build an ecosystem
prompt: Investors are pushing you to build an 'ecosystem' around your product. Define what that actually means for a product of your choice, and what the first three concrete moves are.

title: Defend against an open-source clone
prompt: An open-source clone of your product is getting traction with developers. Walk through the strategic options: ignore, embrace, attack, differentiate. Pick one and defend.

title: Pivot product positioning
prompt: Your product was positioned for one segment but the actual paying users are different. Walk through how you would pivot positioning without alienating the existing base.

title: Disrupt an entrenched incumbent
prompt: You are launching a product against an entrenched incumbent with 10x your distribution. Make the case for a disruption strategy and pick the wedge feature you would build first.

title: PLG vs sales-led for a new product
prompt: A new product can launch product-led (free tier, self-serve) or sales-led (enterprise pilots first). Frame the trade-offs and recommend a path for an early-stage SaaS.

title: Move from prosumer to enterprise
prompt: A successful prosumer product wants to move upmarket to enterprise. Walk through what changes about the product, GTM, and org, and what you would deliberately not change.

title: Manage a declining product line
prompt: A product line is declining 6% year-over-year but still profitable. The CEO asks for a 3-year plan. Walk through harvest vs. invest vs. sunset, and recommend.

title: Distribution partnership trade-offs
prompt: A potential distribution partner could 5x your reach but wants exclusivity in their vertical. Walk through the trade-offs and recommend a structure that you could actually negotiate.
